snippet:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

root = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2), TreeNode(3))
[root.val, root.left.val, root.right.val]
cue: Each node holds a value plus links to a left and right child node.
note: A binary tree is made of nodes. Each node stores `val` and two child links, `left` and `right`. A missing child is None. The top node is the root.

concept: recursion base case on an empty child
cue: The base case stops the recursion: an empty node has depth zero.
note: Almost every tree function recurses on `node.left` and `node.right`. The `if node is None` check is the base case that stops recursion at the empty links below the leaves.

concept: max depth of a small tree
cue: A node's depth is one more than the deeper of its two children.
note: The deepest path here is 3 -> 20 -> 15 (or 3 -> 20 -> 7), three nodes tall.

concept: max depth of a binary tree
prompt: Write `max_depth(root)` that returns the number of nodes on the longest path from the root down to a leaf. An empty tree (root is None) has depth 0. Use recursion.
mapsTo: LC 104 · Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
note: Each node is visited once, so time is O(n). The recursion stack goes as deep as the tree, so extra space is O(h) where h is the height.
cue: Base case returns 0 for None; otherwise one plus the deeper child.

cases.setup: root = TreeNode(3, TreeNode(9), TreeNode(20, TreeNode(15), TreeNode(7)))
root = TreeNode(1)
root = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(3, TreeNode(4))))

cue: Inorder visits the left subtree, then the node, then the right subtree.
note: Inorder means left, node, right. On a binary search tree it yields the values in sorted order.

prompt: Write `inorder(root)` that returns a list of node values visited in inorder: left subtree, then the node, then the right subtree. An empty tree returns an empty list. Use recursion.
note: Every node is appended once, so time is O(n); the recursion stack is O(h) for a tree of height h.
cue: Recurse left, wrap the node value in a one-element list, recurse right, join.

cases.setup: root = TreeNode(2, TreeNode(1), TreeNode(3))
root = TreeNode(1, None, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(3)))
root = TreeNode(4, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(1), TreeNode(3)), TreeNode(6, TreeNode(5), TreeNode(7)))

prompt: Write `preorder(root)` that returns a list of node values visited in preorder: the node first, then the left subtree, then the right subtree. An empty tree returns an empty list. Use recursion.
note: Same O(n) time and O(h) stack space as inorder; only the position of `[root.val]` changes, which changes the visit order.
cue: Put the node value first, then the left list, then the right list.

cases.setup: root = TreeNode(1, None, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(3)))
root = TreeNode(1)
root = TreeNode(4, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(1), TreeNode(3)), TreeNode(6, TreeNode(5), TreeNode(7)))

snippet:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val, left=None, right=None):
        self.val = val
        self.left = left
        self.right = right

root = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2), TreeNode(3))
root.left, root.right = root.right, root.left
[root.left.val, root.right.val]
cue: One tuple assignment swaps the left and right links in place.
note: Mirroring a whole tree is this one swap applied at every node.

prompt: Write `invert(root)` that mirrors the tree by swapping the left and right child of every node, then returns `root`. An empty tree (None) returns None. Use recursion.
note: Each node is swapped once, so time is O(n) and the recursion stack is O(h). After inverting, an inorder walk comes out reversed.
cue: Swap the two children, inverting each subtree as you assign it, then return the node.
Two trees match only if the same positions hold nodes and the values agree.
syntax: p is None and q is None
note: Same values but different shape counts as different: here the 2 hangs on the left in `a` and on the right in `b`.

prompt: Write `is_same(p, q)` that returns True if the two trees have the same shape and the same values at every position, otherwise False. Two empty trees are the same. Use recursion.
note: Time is O(n) over the smaller tree; the recursion stack is O(h). The two None checks handle the base cases before comparing values.
cue: Both None means match; exactly one None means mismatch; else compare values and recurse on both sides.
syntax: p.val == q.val and recurse left and recurse right

cases.setup:
p = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2), TreeNode(3))
q = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2), TreeNode(3))

p = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2))
q = TreeNode(1, None, TreeNode(2))

p = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2), TreeNode(1))
q = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(1), TreeNode(2))

cue: Fixing `len(q)` before the inner loop pins down exactly one level per pass.
note: Breadth-first order uses a queue instead of recursion. Snapshotting `len(q)` at the top of each pass keeps the nodes of one level together before their children are dequeued.

prompt: Write `level_order(root)` that returns a list of levels, where each level is a list of the node values at that depth, from top to bottom and left to right. An empty tree returns an empty list. Use a queue (collections.deque).
mapsTo: LC 102 · Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
note: Each node enters and leaves the queue once, so time is O(n). The queue can hold a whole level, so space is O(w) where w is the widest level.
cue: Start the queue with the root; each pass drains the current level and enqueues its children.

cases.setup: root = TreeNode(3, TreeNode(9), TreeNode(20, TreeNode(15), TreeNode(7)))
root = TreeNode(1)
root = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2, TreeNode(4)), TreeNode(3))

prompt: Write `has_path_sum(root, target)` that returns True if some path from the root down to a leaf has node values that add up to `target`, otherwise False. A leaf is a node with no children. An empty tree returns False. Use recursion, subtracting each node's value as you descend.
note: Each node is checked once, so time is O(n) and the recursion stack is O(h). The sum only counts when you reach a leaf, so a match at a non-leaf node does not qualify.
cue: At a leaf, check whether its value equals what remains; otherwise subtract and recurse into each child.
syntax: root.left is None and root.right is None

cases.setup: root = TreeNode(5, TreeNode(4, TreeNode(11, TreeNode(7), TreeNode(2))), TreeNode(8, TreeNode(13), TreeNode(4, None, TreeNode(1))))
root = TreeNode(5, TreeNode(4, TreeNode(11, TreeNode(7), TreeNode(2))), TreeNode(8, TreeNode(13), TreeNode(4, None, TreeNode(1))))
root = TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2))
root = TreeNode(7)
